dominant_error_pattern: Students select peer delegation (option A), full task exemption (option C), or unilateral teacher-assigned substitution (option D) — all of which bypass the essential step of discussing with the student what accommodations they actually need. The dominant error is prerequisite-skipping: students do not recognize that identifying appropriate accommodations requires a collaborative discussion with the student before any support is implemented.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing option A misapply peer support as an accommodation mechanism — while peer collaboration can be one classroom support tool, it does not substitute for the teacher's responsibility to determine and provide appropriate IEP or 504 accommodations. Students choosing option C hold the misconception that excusing a student from an assignment is an appropriate accommodation; the standard of participation and learning is maintained through accommodations, not eliminated. Students choosing option D are applying unilateral teacher substitution without consulting the student about what accommodations would actually address their visual impairment in this context — students with visual impairment have a range of support needs and must be included in accommodation planning. Teaching move: review the IEP/504 team process and clarify that the student is a key participant in identifying appropriate accommodations.

dominant_error_pattern: Students select generic testing accommodations — extra time (option A) or frequent breaks (option B) — or a vague 'alternative version' (option D) when dysgraphia requires specifically targeting the written motor production barrier, not extending or interrupting the time spent producing it. The dominant error is component-confusion: students apply accommodations that address peripheral testing conditions rather than the core impairment component driving dysgraphia errors.
instructional_red_flags: Students who choose option A have not yet differentiated dysgraphia-specific accommodations from general test accommodations — extra time extends how long the student struggles with motor production but does not reduce or eliminate that demand. Students choosing option B have a similar gap: frequent breaks reduce physical fatigue but do not remove the underlying motor production requirement. Students choosing option D are applying an 'alternative = accommodation' heuristic without specifying whether the alternative removes the written production demand; the critical question for dysgraphia is whether the format eliminates the handwriting or extended writing requirement specifically. Teaching move: have students map each accommodation option to the specific impairment component it targets — distinguishing motor production barriers (dysgraphia) from time-pressure barriers (ADHD, anxiety) and cognitive load barriers (SLD in reading).

dominant_error_pattern: Each wrong answer changes the content or level of what is being read or assessed — substituting picture-pointing for written response (option A), lowering the reading level of the test (option B), or matching reading materials to cognitive ability level (option D) — rather than addressing the fluency-specific time-pressure barrier while preserving the grade-level reading expectation. The dominant error is model-conflation: students select changes to what is read rather than to the conditions under which reading occurs, conflating reading content modifications with reading fluency accommodations.
instructional_red_flags: Students choosing option A have selected a task-substitution that removes the reading component entirely rather than accommodating the fluency barrier — picture-pointing is not an accommodation for reading fluency but an exemption from reading. Students choosing option B are applying a reading-level modification when a condition-of-access accommodation is what the question describes; lowering the test level changes the standard, not the time available to meet it. Students choosing option D conflate reading fluency (rate and automaticity) with cognitive ability level — a student can have strong cognitive ability and a significant fluency impairment; matching material to 'cognitive ability level' may under-challenge the student or mismatch the intervention to the actual barrier. Teaching move: have students distinguish the three distinct reading constructs — comprehension, fluency, and decoding — and match each to its corresponding accommodation versus modification strategy.
